question:
in the first quadrant as in the figure. Find $c$ so that the areas of the
two shaded regions are equal. [Figure not included. The first region is
bounded by the $y$-axis, the line $y=c$ and the curve; the other lies
under the curve and above the line $y=c$ between their two points of
intersection.]
solution:
Solution. Let \( (b, c) \) denote the rightmost intersection point. (See Figure 23.) We wish to find \( c \) such that
\[
\int_{0}^{b}\left(\left(2 x-3 x^{3}\right)-c\right) d x=0 .
\]

This leads to \( b^{2}-(3 / 4) b^{4}-b c=0 \). After substituting \( c=2 b-3 b^{3} \) and solving, we find a unique positive solution, namely \( b=2 / 3 \). Thus \( c=4 / 9 \). To validate the solution, we check that \( (2 / 3,4 / 9) \) is rightmost among the intersection points of \( y=4 / 9 \) and \( y=2 x-3 x^{3} \) : the zeros of \( 2 x-3 x^{3}-4 / 9=(2 / 3-x)\left(3 x^{2}+2 x-2 / 3\right) \) other than \( 2 / 3 \) are \( (-1 \pm \sqrt{3}) / 3 \), which are less than \( 2 / 3 \).

descriptive_long.question:
in the first quadrant as in the figure. Find $charlieparam$ so that the areas of the
two shaded regions are equal. [Figure not included. The first region is
bounded by the $alphayvariable$-axis, the line $alphayvariable=charlieparam$ and the curve; the other lies
under the curve and above the line $alphayvariable=charlieparam$ between their two points of
intersection.]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( (\text{betaparam}, \text{charlieparam}) \) denote the rightmost intersection point. (See Figure 23.) We wish to find \( \text{charlieparam} \) such that
\[
\int_{0}^{\text{betaparam}}\left(\left(2 \text{alphaxvariable}-3 \text{alphaxvariable}^{3}\right)-\text{charlieparam}\right) d \text{alphaxvariable}=0 .
\]

This leads to \( \text{betaparam}^{2}-(3 / 4) \text{betaparam}^{4}-\text{betaparam}\,\text{charlieparam}=0 \). After substituting \( \text{charlieparam}=2 \text{betaparam}-3 \text{betaparam}^{3} \) and solving, we find a unique positive solution, namely \( \text{betaparam}=2 / 3 \). Thus \( \text{charlieparam}=4 / 9 \). To validate the solution, we check that \( (2 / 3,4 / 9) \) is rightmost among the intersection points of \( \text{alphayvariable}=4 / 9 \) and \( \text{alphayvariable}=2 \text{alphaxvariable}-3 \text{alphaxvariable}^{3} \) : the zeros of \( 2 \text{alphaxvariable}-3 \text{alphaxvariable}^{3}-4 / 9=(2 / 3-\text{alphaxvariable})\left(3 \text{alphaxvariable}^{2}+2 \text{alphaxvariable}-2 / 3\right) \) other than \( 2 / 3 \) are \( (-1 \pm \sqrt{3}) / 3 \), which are less than \( 2 / 3 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: in the first quadrant as in the figure. Find $notebook$ so that the areas of the two shaded regions are equal. [Figure not included. The first region is bounded by the $pineapple$-axis, the line $pineapple=notebook$ and the curve; the other lies under the curve and above the line $pineapple=notebook$ between their two points of intersection.]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( (dinosaur, notebook) \) denote the rightmost intersection point. (See Figure 23.) We wish to find \( notebook \) such that
\[
\int_{0}^{dinosaur}\left(\left(2 waterfall-3 waterfall^{3}\right)-notebook\right) d waterfall=0 .
\]

This leads to \( dinosaur^{2}-(3 / 4) dinosaur^{4}-dinosaur\,notebook=0 \). After substituting \( notebook=2 dinosaur-3 dinosaur^{3} \) and solving, we find a unique positive solution, namely \( dinosaur=2 / 3 \). Thus \( notebook=4 / 9 \). To validate the solution, we check that \( (2 / 3,4 / 9) \) is rightmost among the intersection points of \( pineapple=4 / 9 \) and \( pineapple=2 waterfall-3 waterfall^{3} \) : the zeros of \( 2 waterfall-3 waterfall^{3}-4 / 9=(2 / 3-waterfall)\left(3 waterfall^{2}+2 waterfall-2 / 3\right) \) other than \( 2 / 3 \) are \( (-1 \pm \sqrt{3}) / 3 \), which are less than \( 2 / 3 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
in the first quadrant as in the figure. Find $variablevalue$ so that the areas of the
two shaded regions are equal. [Figure not included. The first region is
bounded by the $horizontalaxis$-axis, the line $horizontalaxis=variablevalue$ and the curve; the other lies
under the curve and above the line $horizontalaxis=variablevalue$ between their two points of
intersection.]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( (leftmostpoint, variablevalue) \) denote the rightmost intersection point. (See Figure 23.) We wish to find \( variablevalue \) such that
\[
\int_{0}^{leftmostpoint}\left(\left(2 knownvalue-3 knownvalue^{3}\right)-variablevalue\right) d knownvalue=0 .
\]

This leads to \( leftmostpoint^{2}-(3 / 4) leftmostpoint^{4}-leftmostpoint variablevalue=0 \). After substituting \( variablevalue=2 leftmostpoint-3 leftmostpoint^{3} \) and solving, we find a unique positive solution, namely \( leftmostpoint=2 / 3 \). Thus \( variablevalue=4 / 9 \). To validate the solution, we check that \( (2 / 3,4 / 9) \) is rightmost among the intersection points of \( horizontalaxis=4 / 9 \) and \( horizontalaxis=2 knownvalue-3 knownvalue^{3} \) : the zeros of \( 2 knownvalue-3 knownvalue^{3}-4 / 9=(2 / 3-knownvalue)\left(3 knownvalue^{2}+2 knownvalue-2 / 3\right) \) other than \( 2 / 3 \) are \( (-1 \pm \sqrt{3}) / 3 \), which are less than \( 2 / 3 \).

garbled_string.question:
in the first quadrant as in the figure. Find $tpkasvud$ so that the areas of the
two shaded regions are equal. [Figure not included. The first region is
bounded by the $hjgrksla$-axis, the line $hjgrksla=tpkasvud$ and the curve; the other lies
under the curve and above the line $hjgrksla=tpkasvud$ between their two points of
intersection.]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( (mncfleor, tpkasvud) \) denote the rightmost intersection point. (See Figure 23.) We wish to find \( tpkasvud \) such that
\[
\int_{0}^{mncfleor}\left(\left(2 qzxwvtnp-3 qzxwvtnp^{3}\right)-tpkasvud\right) d qzxwvtnp=0 .
\]

This leads to \( mncfleor^{2}-(3 / 4) mncfleor^{4}-mncfleor tpkasvud=0 \). After substituting \( tpkasvud=2 mncfleor-3 mncfleor^{3} \) and solving, we find a unique positive solution, namely \( mncfleor=2 / 3 \). Thus \( tpkasvud=4 / 9 \). To validate the solution, we check that \( (2 / 3,4 / 9) \) is rightmost among the intersection points of \( hjgrksla=4 / 9 \) and \( hjgrksla=2 qzxwvtnp-3 qzxwvtnp^{3} \) : the zeros of \( 2 qzxwvtnp-3 qzxwvtnp^{3}-4 / 9=(2 / 3-qzxwvtnp)\left(3 qzxwvtnp^{2}+2 qzxwvtnp-2 / 3\right) \) other than \( 2 / 3 \) are \( (-1 \pm \sqrt{3}) / 3 \), which are less than \( 2 / 3 \).

kernel_variant.question:
In the first quadrant consider the cubic curve
\[
\gamma:\; y = 9x-2x^{3}.
\]
Let the vertical line \(x=1\) and an as-yet-unknown horizontal line \(y=c\) cut the curve as shown.  Denote by \(b>1\) the abscissa of the right-hand intersection of the horizontal line with \(\gamma\).
Two planar regions are shaded:
  (i) the set of points lying between the curve, the line \(x=1\) and the line \(y=c\);  
  (ii) the set of points that lie under the curve and above the line \(y=c\) between the two intersection points of \(y=c\) with the curve.
Find the number \(c>0\) for which the two shaded regions have equal area.
kernel_variant.solution:
Corrected Solution.  Let f(x)=9x-2x^3.  We seek c>0 and abscissae 1<a<b in the first quadrant with f(a)=f(b)=c such that

Region I: the set of points between the vertical line x=1, the curve y=f(x), and the horizontal line y=c, and
Region II: the set of points between the curve y=f(x) and the line y=c for x running from a to b,
have equal area.

1.  Area of Region I.  On [1,a] one has f(x)<c, so the shaded vertical strip has area
   S_1 = \int _1^a (c - f(x)) dx
   = \int _1^a (c - (9x-2x^3)) dx.

2.  Area of Region II.  On [a,b] one has f(x)>c, so
   S_2 = \int _a^b (f(x) - c) dx
   = \int _a^b ((9x-2x^3) - c) dx.

3.  Equating S_1=S_2 gives
   \int _1^a (c-f(x)) dx = \int _a^b (f(x)-c) dx
   \Rightarrow  \int _1^b (f(x)-c) dx =0.

4.  But f(b)=c, so c = 9b - 2b^3.  Also
   \int _1^b f(x) dx = \int _1^b (9x-2x^3) dx
    = [ (9/2)x^2 - (1/2)x^4 ]_1^b
    = (9/2 b^2 - 1/2 b^4) - (9/2 - 1/2)
    = 9/2 b^2 - 1/2 b^4 - 4.

   Hence \int _1^b(f-c)dx = (9/2 b^2 - 1/2 b^4 - 4) - c(b-1) = 0
   \Rightarrow  multiply by 2 and substitute c=9b-2b^3:
     9b^2 - b^4 - 8 - 2(9b-2b^3)(b-1) = 0
   \Rightarrow  3b^4 -4b^3 -9b^2 +18b -8 = 0.
   Factor: (b-1)^2(3b^2+2b-8)=0.  Discard b=1 double-root; solve 3b^2+2b-8=0:
     b = (-2 \pm  \sqrt{100})/6 \Rightarrow  b = 4/3 (positive).

5.  Finally c = f(b) = 9\cdot (4/3) - 2\cdot (4/3)^3 = 12 - 128/27 = 196/27.

Answer:  c = 196/27.

Remark.  One checks that f'(x)=9-6x^2 has its maximum at x=\sqrt{3/2}\approx 1.225<4/3, so the intersection at x=4/3 is indeed the unique right-most one, and the one at x=a\in (1,\sqrt{3/2}).

core_steps: Express “equality of shaded areas” as ∫(curve − c) dx = 0 between the two x-intersections.
Call the right intersection abscissa b and use the intersection condition c = f(b).
Insert c into the area equation to get a single algebraic equation in b and solve for the positive root.
Verify that this (b, c) is indeed the rightmost intersection of the line and the curve.

slot1.description: Linear coefficient of the cubic curve y = αx − βx³

slot2.description: Magnitude of the (negative) cubic coefficient of the curve

slot3.description: Vertical line that serves as the left boundary of the region

slot4.description: Positive abscissa of the rightmost intersection point

slot5.description: Height of the horizontal line whose position is sought
